coredata.dc:description: Spent coffee ground is a solid waste deriving from the production of coffee beverages and instant coffee in high amounts worldwide. The recycling of spent coffee ground is an approach to support the UN sustainable development goals (UN-SDGs) regarding waste reduction and recycling. The objective of this study was to synthesize carbon quantum dot (CQD) from the spent coffee ground using the alkaline peroxide-assisted hydrothermal method, and subsequently produce the composite photocatalysts by combining ZnO and TiO2 with CQD. The obtained CQD has an average particle size of 2.96 nm. The optical bandgap energies decreased after combining TiO2 and ZnO with CQD. TEM and XPS results confirmed the formation of composite photocatalysts. All photocatalysts were used in the removal of carbaryl (5 mg/L) in water under UVA irradiation (365 nm). The ternary composite of TiO2/ZnO/CQD exhibited the highest removal efficiency of 99.01 % under UVA irradiation for 90 min. After reusing the catalyst for five cycles, the efficiency dropped to 84.09 %. The half-life time of carbaryl in the presence of TiO2/ZnO/CQD under UVA was 12.16 min. This is 5.5 and 1.7 times lower than that of pristine TiO2 and ZnO. When the initial concentration of carbaryl increased from 1 to 7 mg/L, the removal efficiency showed a decreasing trend, which was expressed in terms of Langmuir-Hinshelwood rate equation. Furthermore, antimicrobial studies were conducted against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial strains. The results demonstrated that TiO2/ZnO/CQD is one of the promising photocatalysts for environmental remediation and antimicrobial applications.

XRD patterns of TiO2, ZnO, TiO2/ZnO, TiO2/CQD, ZnO/CQD, and TiO2/ZnO/CQD.

(a) Absorption spectra of TiO2, ZnO, TiO2/ZnO, TiO2/CQD, ZnO/CQD, and TiO2/ZnO/CQD, (b) Tauc plot for direct bandgap, (c) Tauc plot for indirect bandgap, and (d) PL spectra of TiO2, ZnO and TiO2/ZnO/CQD.

(a) Antimicrobial activity for TiO2/ZnO/CQD using agar well-diffusion method against E. coli, and S. aureus bacterial strains and (b) Bar diagram depicting the zone of inhibition (mm) of TiO2/ZnO/CQD against various concentrations.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Spent coffee ground is a solid waste deriving from the production of coffee beverages and instant coffee in high amounts worldwide. The recycling of spent coffee ground is an approach to support the UN sustainable development goals (UN-SDGs) regarding waste reduction and recycling. The objective of this study was to synthesize carbon quantum dot (CQD) from the spent coffee ground using the alkaline peroxide-assisted hydrothermal method, and subsequently produce the composite photocatalysts by combining ZnO and TiO2 with CQD. The obtained CQD has an average particle size of 2.96 nm. The optical bandgap energies decreased after combining TiO2 and ZnO with CQD. TEM and XPS results confirmed the formation of composite photocatalysts. All photocatalysts were used in the removal of carbaryl (5 mg/L) in water under UVA irradiation (365 nm). The ternary composite of TiO2/ZnO/CQD exhibited the highest removal efficiency of 99.01 % under UVA irradiation for 90 min. After reusing the catalyst for five cycles, the efficiency dropped to 84.09 %. The half-life time of carbaryl in the presence of TiO2/ZnO/CQD under UVA was 12.16 min. This is 5.5 and 1.7 times lower than that of pristine TiO2 and ZnO. When the initial concentration of carbaryl increased from 1 to 7 mg/L, the removal efficiency showed a decreasing trend, which was expressed in terms of Langmuir-Hinshelwood rate equation. Furthermore, antimicrobial studies were conducted against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial strains. The results demonstrated that TiO2/ZnO/CQD is one of the promising photocatalysts for environmental remediation and antimicrobial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Coffee is one of the most popular beverages, especially in modern society. The coffee consumption trends significantly lead to the expansion of coffee market worldwide [1]. Spent coffee ground (SCG) is organic solid residue obtained from the production of coffee beverages and instant coffee. SCG is usually discarded as solid waste, which is finally burned or sent to the sanitary landfill [2,3]. The global SCG generation is estimated at 6 million tons annually. On average, each ton of raw coffee bean generates about 650 kg of spent coffee ground during the industrial production of instant coffee [2]. The recycling of SCG is becoming a topic of concern relating to sustainable development goals (SDGs) because it helps to reduce the quantity of waste in the landfill and minimize the contamination of chemicals (e.g., caffeine) in soil and groundwater. There are various practical ways to reuse and recycle SCG. The SCG can be used as a source of value-added products, including biodiesel, brick, activated carbon, fertilizer and soil amendment [1,2]. Additionally, it can be used as carbon source for synthesis of carbon quantum dot, which had great potential to use for bioimaging [4], chemo-sensing [5], heavy metal detection [4,6], and photocatalytic removal of dye [7] and pharmaceuticals (i.e., caffeine, carbamazepine, and ibuprofen) from water [8].

Carbon quantum dot (CQD) is a new type of carbon-based luminescent nanomaterial, which has diameter smaller than 10 nm [9]. The outstanding properties of CQD include easy production, high water solubility, excellent photostability, biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness [10,11]. CQD has a wide range of applications such as photocatalysis, energy conversion, heavy metals sensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery [12]. CQD can be synthesized from various raw materials that contain significant carbon contents, including organic chemicals (e.g., citric acid, ethylene diamine, and urea) and organic wastes (e.g., dead leaves, corncob, banana peel, and spent coffee ground) [13]. CQD can be synthesized by various methods such as hydrothermal method, solvothermal method, laser ablation, microwave irradiation, pyrolysis, electrochemical oxidation, and ultrasonication. Most of the studies have used hydrothermal methods to synthesize CQD because it can provide high quantity and quality of CQD with bright blue photoluminescence under UV irradiation (360–365 nm) [12]. Hydrothermal method is an easy route to synthesize the CQD by heating carbon-based raw material in the reaction medium (e.g., deionized water, acidic solution, alkaline solution).

Semiconductor photocatalysis is an advanced oxidation process to convert organic contaminants into harmless substances, CO2, and H2O [14]. Conventional metal oxide semiconductors are widely used as photocatalyst, such as zinc oxide (ZnO), titanium dioxide (TiO2), Copper (I) oxide (Cu2O), bismuth oxide (Bi2O3), cerium oxide (CeO2), and many others [15,16]. Photocatalytic process involves the excitation of electrons from valence band to conduction band by irradiation of light over the photocatalyst, which generates photo-excited electrons and holes [17,18]. The electrons and holes can undergo redox reactions to produce the reactive oxygen species (ROS) including hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radical (
O 2
• −
), which have potential to degrade various organic pollutants [15]. Unfortunately, the application of metal oxides is still limited because electrons and holes are easy to recombine resulting in low photocatalytic activity [17,18]. Fabrication of the composite photocatalyst is one of the best approaches to reduce charge carrier recombination, and thereby enhance the photocatalytic performance [19].

Most of the previous studies regarding photocatalytic application of CQD focused on the fabrication of TiO2/CQD and ZnO/CQD composite photocatalysts for water treatment. The target organic pollutants were dye and pharmaceutical compounds [7,8,20–22]. The ternary composite of TiO2 /ZnO/CQD was reported. Organic chemicals including citric acid and polyethyleneimine were used as raw materials for hydrothermal synthesis of CQD. The ternary composite of TiO2/ZnO/CQD showed good photocatalytic activity for the removal of methylene blue (96.7 %) from water [23]. In terms of water pollution and water treatment, pesticides are listed as contaminants of emerging concern, which are increasingly detected in the water bodies because they are resistant to conventional wastewater treatment process [24,25]. Carbaryl (1-naphtyl methyl carbamate; C12H11NO2) is a carbamate pesticide, which is commonly used to control insects in farms, fields, and golf courses [26]. It is known to be very toxic to humans and animals [24]. With the global spread of carbaryl usage, it has been detected in various environmental compartments including surface water, groundwater, soil, aquatic living organisms, and even in drinking water [27,28]. The photodegradation (direct and indirect) of carbaryl in river water under simulated solar irradiation have been reported [28]. The overall photodegradation rate constant (k) of carbaryl in river water was 6.5 × 10−5 s−1. For indirect photodegradation, carbaryl was attacked by the reactive oxygen species (ROS), which produced upon solar irradiation, including hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2). The calculated half-life times of carbaryl in river water were 3.6 days with •OH and 17.06 days with 1O2. Although the carbaryl can be naturally transformed through photodegradation by sunlight [28], the residues of carbaryl can pose the negative impacts including neurotoxicity, reproductive toxicity, and endocrine-disrupting effect on the living organisms [26].

Therefore, it is necessary to develop photocatalytic material, which can effectively degrade carbaryl from water. Hence, the present study hypothesized that the synergistic effects of spent coffee ground-derived CQD, ZnO and TiO2 in its ternary composite photocatalyst could enhance the photocatalytic performance. The novelty of this study includes: (i) CQD derived from organic waste (i.e., spent coffee ground) was synthesized using a facile method, which requires lower heating temperature, lower energy consumption, and shorter time as compared to the previous work [8], (ii) CQD-based photocatalysts were used for the removal of hazardous substances (i.e., carbaryl insecticide) from water and antibacterial application, and (iii) the mechanisms of CQD derived from spent coffee ground, ZnO and TiO2 in its ternary composite photocatalyst were clearly described. The advantages of using spent coffee ground to synthesize the CQD through a facile method are cost-effectiveness and environmental sustainability, which can contribute to the concepts of zero-waste and circular economy because it was upcycled into value-added product.

The aim of this study is to synthesize CQD from the spent coffee ground using the alkaline peroxide-assisted hydrothermal method, and subsequently produce the composite photocatalysts by combining ZnO and TiO2 with CQD through a facile thermal treatment. Various characterization techniques including the transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), scanning electron microscopy and energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDS), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were studied to describe the physical and chemical properties of the photocatalysts. Performance of the composite photocatalysts was investigated through photocatalytic removal of carbaryl insecticides in water. In addition, the antibacterial properties were also tested to determine the potential application for infectious control. The antibacterial capabilities were tested against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus).

Spent coffee ground (Arabica) was collected from a coffee shop in Bangkok, Thailand, and then dried at 105 °C for 24 h. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 % w/v) was purchased from Carlo Erba Reagents. Sodium hydroxide pellete (NaOH, 99 %) was purchased from QRëC. Dichloromethane (99.8 %) was purchased from RCI Labscan. Titanium dioxide nanopowder (TiO2, 95 %) and zinc oxide powder (ZnO, 99 %) were purchased from Srichem. Carbaryl insecticides was purchased from ERAWAN Agricultural Chemical (Thailand). All chemical reagents were used as received without any purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon-quantum dot solution from spent coffee ground
ce:section.ce:para: The CQD solution was prepared by the alkaline peroxide-assisted hydrothermal method. 15 g of the dried coffee ground was added into 400 mL of 2 M NaOH solution. After stirring for 1 h, 100 mL of H2O2 was added into the mixture and then put into the pressure vessel with a capacity of 1 L. Subsequently, the vessel was sealed and installed with the high-pressure hydrothermal reactor. The temperature was set at 300 °C for 4 h under stirring of 60 rpm. After cooling down, the mixture was filtrated with 6 μm and 0.22 μm filter papers. The supernatant solution was then purified by dichloromethane using a separatory funnel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot composite with metal oxides
ce:section.ce:para: The TiO2/CQD, ZnO/CQD, and TiO2/ZnO/CQD were prepared by a facile heat-treatment method. Firstly, 120 mL of deionized water was mixed with 4 g of TiO2 for TiO2/CQD, 4 g of ZnO for ZnO/CQD, 2 g of TiO2 and 2 g of ZnO for TiO2/ZnO/CQD. Next, the mixtures were individually stirred under room temperature for 30 min. After that 20 mL of CQD solution was added into each mixture, and stirred for another 30 min. Each mixture was then heated at 150 °C for 12 h in the hot air oven. The obtained solid was pasted to the powder form and kept in the desiccator. TiO2/ZnO also prepared using the same procedure as described above without the addition of CQD solution.

For the synthesized CQD solution, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured by the JEOL JEM-2100 model with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The absorbance spectrum was scanned using UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Model U-3900) in the range of 250–700 nm. The fluorescence property of CQD solution was analyzed by fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Model LS55). For the photocatalytic materials, TEM images were captured by the JEOL JEM-2100 plus. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern and crystallite phase were analyzed by AERIS high-resolution benchtop. The XRD data were collected from 2-theta of 10° to 80° with an interval of 0.01. The absorbance and reflectance spectra were scanned using UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS, Ocean optics PX2) in the range of 200–800 nm. Photoluminescence behavior was measured using photoluminescence spectrometer (HORIBA Scientific, model: FLUOROMAX PLUS) with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm and emission wavelength of 400–700 nm. The surface area, pore size, and pore volume were analyzed by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analyzer (Model BELSORP MAX, Japan). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm was obtained at 77 K. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL: model JSM-5410LV) and energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS model INCA-350) were used to analyze the surface morphology and elemental composition of the photocatalyst. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with an Al Ka X-ray source was used to analyze the chemical state and chemical composition.

The experiments were conducted in a batch reactor as illustrated in Fig. S1. 100 mL of carbaryl solution and 0.1 g of photocatalyst were added in each Erlenmeyer flask. The initial concentration of carbaryl was fixed at 5 mg/L to determine the effect of photocatalyst types. Subsequently, the optimal photocatalyst was selected to determine the effect of initial concentrations of carbaryl (1, 3, 5, 7 mg/L) and reusability test. Light intensity was fixed at 1.12 mW/cm2 for all photocatalytic experiments. The experiment was initially run in the presence of photocatalysts under the darkness for 30 min to assure the equilibrium adsorption-desorption of carbaryl on the surface of photocatalysts. After that, the UVA light bulbs (Osram, 300 W) were switched on for another 90 min. Water samples were collected at the desired periods, and then filtrated with 0.22 μm nylon syringe filter. The concentration of carbaryl in water samples were analyzed using UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Model U-3900). The maximum absorption of carbaryl was observed at 220 nm. The absorption peak was chosen to evaluate the residual carbaryl concentration in water samples.

To identify the effect of initial pH, the pH range of 3–11 was adjusted using 3 M of sodium hydroxide (NaoH) and 3 M of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) for alkaline and acidic condition, respectively. To identify the reactive oxygen species (ROSs) involved, specific trapping experiments were conducted. Various scavengers, including isopropanol (IPA), edetate disodium (EDTA-2Na), benzoquinone (BQ), and silver nitrate (AgNO3), were introduced into the reaction mixture prior to the addition of photocatalyst. In the reusability test, the photocatalyst powder was recovered by centrifugation followed by filtration using membrane filter (0.22 μm). The separated photocatalyst was washed with deionized water, dried at 70 °C for 24 h, milled to powder form, and used for the next cycle.

TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite was evaluated for their antibacterial properties against two bacterial strains using the agar well diffusion method. The current study included two types of microorganisms: Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC6538) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922). To prepare the culture plates, a sterilized glass rod was used to spread 100 μL of a 24 h old broth culture of the bacterial strains. A sterile cork borer was then used to punch wells in each Petri dish. The wells were filled with two concentrations (50 and 75 μg/mL) of the TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to allow the formation of inhibitory zones around the wells. The diameters of these zones were measured and recorded to determine the antibacterial efficacy of the TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite. The widths of the inhibition zones were determined and documented to determine the antimicrobial efficiency of TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite. Ciprofloxacin, a commonly used antibiotic, was employed as a standard for assessment. The zones of inhibition were detected and measured in mm. Three replicates were performed for each sample, and the mean results were calculated to determine the antimicrobial activity of the TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of CQD solution is shown in Fig. S2. The absorption spectrum band between 250 and 300 nm was ascribed to π- π* transition of the aromatic CC bond [5,22]. The optical bandgap of the CQD solution was estimated by Kubelka-Munk equation coupled with Tauc plot method for direct bandgap semiconductor [29]. Upon plotting the (αhv)2 versus photon energy (Inset of Fig. S2), the optical bandgap can be obtained by extrapolating the linear fit to the intercept of the energy axis. Α, h, and v represent the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and frequency of photon, respectively. The optical bandgap energy of the CQD solution was estimated to be 1.85 eV.

The fluorescence property of CQD solution was investigated by UV excitation at 365 nm, which corresponds to the wavelength of UV lamps in photocatalytic experiments. From Fig. 1a, the synthesized CQD solution was light brown under visible light but exhibited strong greenish-blue emission under UV (365 nm). The maximum emission was observed at 539 nm. With the increase of excitation wavelengths from 360 to 500 nm (Fig. S3), the maximum emission wavelengths were different. The emission behavior showed down-conversion photoluminescence. The emission wavelength is dependent on the excitation wavelength, particle size of CQD, and the surface functional groups [30,31]. The blue and green emission CQD were synthesized by hydrothermal method using various carbon sources such as jatropha fruit, human hair, citric acid, sewage sludge, denatured milk, water hyacinth and cauliflower. The particle sizes of CQD ranged from 2 to 11 nm [13].

TEM image of the synthesized CQD solution is shown in Fig. 1b. CQD particles were spherical shape and highly monodispersed without any aggregation. The average diameter of the CQD particle was 2.96 ± 0.85 nm (estimated from 30 particles in TEM image). The zoomed-in images of single CQD exhibited the lattice spacing of 0.204 and 0.215 nm, indicating the presence of sp2 (1120) graphitic crystal phase of graphene. These results are consistent with the previous study. The CQD solution was synthesized from cabbage by hydrothermal method. The CQD particles were found to be 2–6 nm in diameter. The crystalline structure had the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm [32].

The crystalline structure and phase composition of the synthesized catalysts were analyzed using XRD, and the resulting patterns are illustrated in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern of pure TiO2 reveals distinct diffraction peaks at approximately 2θ values of 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.0°, and 54.0°, which are indicative of the anatase phase (JCPDS card No. 21–1272). Similarly, the XRD pattern of pure ZnO exhibits pronounced peaks at 2θ values of 31.7°, 34.4°, 36.2°, 47.5°, 56.6°, 62.8°, and 67.9° corresponding to the hexagonal wurtzite phase (JCPDS card No. 36–1451), thereby confirming the high crystallinity of ZnO. In the TiO2/ZnO composite, the diffraction peaks corresponding to anatase TiO2 (denoted by ▲) and ZnO (denoted by ◆) are distinctly observable. This indicates the successful formation of a binary composite without any additional impurity phases. The incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the TiO2, ZnO, and TiO2/ZnO systems (TiO2/CQD, ZnO/CQD, and TiO2/ZnO/CQD) did not alter the fundamental crystalline phases of either TiO2 or ZnO. This finding suggests that the addition of CQDs does not compromise the structural integrity of the TiO2/ZnO composite. However, the characteristic peak associated with CQDs, typically observed around 2θ ≈ 26°, is not prominently detected. This absence can be attributed to the amorphous nature of CQDs and their comparatively low diffraction intensity relative to the highly crystalline phases of TiO2 and ZnO. The lack of additional peaks in the composite systems further confirms their phase purity and the effective incorporation of CQDs. [33–35].

Fundamentally, ZnO has a direct bandgap in wurtzite structure, whereas TiO2 has an indirect bandgap in anatase structure [36,37]. Therefore, the direct and indirect bandgaps were estimated based on the composition of each photocatalysts. Fig. 3a presents the absorption spectra of all photocatalysts. The optical bandgap energies were estimated using Kubelka-Munk equation coupled with Tauc method [29], as shown in Fig. 3b-c. The estimated bandgap energies of all samples are listed in Table 1 . The optical bandgap energies of pristine TiO2 and ZnO were 3.04 and 3.12 eV, corresponding to the absorption edges of 408 and 394 nm, respectively. After composite with CQD, the optical bandgap energies of TiO2/CQD and ZnO/CQD were slightly decreased. The ternary composite of TiO2/ZnO/CQD exhibited the direct and indirect bandgaps of 2.92 and 2.70 eV corresponding to 425 and 459 nm, respectively. This significant bandgap narrowing in TiO2/ZnO/CQD highlights its enhanced light-harvesting capabilities compared to pristine TiO2 and ZnO.

The PL spectra of TiO2, ZnO and TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposites were analyzed to assess their charge carrier recombination dynamics, as shown in Fig. 3d. These spectra provide valuable insights into carrier dynamics such as trapping, migration, and interfacial charge transfer, which are essential for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. TiO2 and ZnO exhibit significantly higher PL intensities, indicating strong electron-hole recombination rates in these materials. In contrast, the TiO2/ZnO/CQD composite has much lower PL intensity, which indicates improved charge separation and less recombination. The suppression is due to the synergistic interaction of TiO2 and ZnO, as well as the presence of CQDs that enhance the transmission of photogenerated electrons. The decrease in PL intensity emphasizes the composite's increased photocatalytic performance, since efficient charge separation is a vital element in increasing photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the integration of CQDs is anticipated to improve visible light absorption and provide additional active sites for photocatalytic processes.

TEM images provide additional information about the structural properties of TiO2/ZnO/COD nanocomposite. The low-magnification TEM image (Fig. 4a) reveals aggregated nanostructures, which are indicative of strong inter-particle interactions due to the high surface energy of the nanoparticles. The observed particle size is approximately 30–40 nm, confirming the successful synthesis of the nanocomposite. The characteristic lattice spacing of TiO2, ZnO, and CQD were observed in the high-resolution TEM image (HRTEM) (Fig. 4b). The lattices spacing of 0.353 nm belong to the (101) plane of anatase [38]. The wurtzite ZnO (101) exhibited the lattice fringe of 0.258 and 0.263 nm. Additionally, the lattice spacing of 0.208 and 0.213 corresponded well with the (100) plane of carbon, which indicates the presence of CQD [39]. These findings confirm the effective integration of TiO2, ZnO, and CQDs in the nanocomposite, revealing its crystalline nature and strong heterostructure. Such structural features are crucial for enhancing photocatalytic activity by promoting efficient charge transfer and reducing electron-hole recombination.

The BET surface area of ZnO, TiO2 and TiO2/ZnO/CQD was evaluated through the isotherm of nitrogen adsorption-desorption (Fig. 5a). The TiO2/ZnO/CQD showed a type IV isotherm, which indicates that it has mesoporous structure (2–50 nm) [38]. The BET surface area was estimated to be 10.64 m2/g, 11.44 m2/g and 24.148 m2/g. The BET adsorption-desorption isotherm results indicates that the TiO2/ZnO/CQD composite exhibits the highest adsorption desorption volume as compared with ZnO and TiO2, thus indicates that enhanced surface area and porosity due to the synergistic interaction between the single components. The pore volume and pore diameter of the ZnO, TiO2 and TiO2/ZnO/CQD was found to be 0.014 cm3/g, 0.019 cm3/g, 0.039 cm3/g, and 5.27 nm, 6.67 nm, 6.461 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the pore volume of ZnO, TiO2, and TiO2/ZnO/CQD were found to be 0.014 cm3/g, 0.019 cm3/g, 0.039 cm3/g, respectively. Similarly, the pore diameter of ZnO, TiO₂, and TiO₂/ZnO/CQD were found to be 5.27 nm, 6.67 nm, and 6.461 nm, respectively.

The BJH distribution of pore size is shown in Fig. 5b. The average pore sizes distribution of ZnO, TiO2, and TiO2/ZnO/CQD were found to be 2.44 nm, 3.295 nm, and 2.501 nm, respectively. The obtained pore size distribution results reveal that TiO2/ZnO/CQD has wider distribution of pore diameters, with a peak in the mesoporous range around 2.51 nm. The ZnO and TiO2 materials also exhibit mesoporosity, though their pore volume and distribution are less pronounced. The enhanced mesoporosity in TiO2/ZnO/CQD is likely due to the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which introduce additional interfacial voids and structural features. This, in turn, increases the surface area and enhances both photocatalytic and antibacterial activity.

The surface chemical states and binding energies of TiO2/ZnO/CQD were discussed from the XPS spectra. The full survey spectrum (Fig. 6a) indicated the presence of Ti, Zn, O, and C in the TiO2/ZnO/CQD composite. The high-resolution spectra of Ti 2p, Zn 2p, C 1 s, and O1s are shown in Fig. 6b-d, respectively. For Ti 2p, the deconvoluted peaks at 457.85 and 464.08 eV are related to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, respectively [40]. For Zn 2p, the peaks located at the binding energies of 1020.6 and 1043.75 are ascribed to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of ZnO, respectively [41]. The deconvoluted peaks of O 1 s indicate the presence of ZnO and TiO bonds at around 529.05 and 530.62 eV, respectively [41,42]. The C 1 s peaks of 284.95 and 288.07 eV are related to the CC and CO bonds, implying the presence of CQD in the composite photocatalyst, which is well consistent with the TEM result. There are no peaks of the TiC and ZnC bonds. Therefore, the CQD does not act as a dopant in the composites. It can be concluded that the TiO2/ZnO/CQD composite was formed by closely electrostatic interaction of the CQD, TiO2, and ZnO [40]. The surface atomic ratio of TiO2/ZnO/CQD are summarized in Table 2 , which can be applied to conduct further experiments.

The objective of this section was to evaluate and compare the removal of carbaryl through the UV irradiation over different types of photocatalytic materials. The blank test without any photocatalyst was first investigated under UVA irradiation to examine the possible photolytic degradation of carbaryl in water. Fig. S4a presents the plots of measured carbaryl concentrations versus reaction time under UVA irradiation. The blank test without any photocatalyst showed the removal efficiency of less than 5 %. Similar behavior has already been reported [28]. The removal of the small amount of carbaryl in this experiment can be explained that carbaryl can absorb photons from UVA lamp, and undergo chemical reactions including cleavage of chemical bonds, oxidation, and hydrolysis [28]. By comparison, the experiment was conducted in the presence of different photocatalysts. The overall removal efficiency of carbaryl regarding the dark adsorption stage combined with photocatalyst activation stage was observed in the sequence of: TiO2/ZnO/CQD (99.01) > ZnO/CQD (95.11 %) > TiO2/ZnO (92.31 %) > TiO2/CQD (91.65 %) > ZnO (88.57 %) > TiO2 (77.01 %). In the presence of TiO2/ZnO/CQD, the absorption spectra of carbaryl as a function of reaction time are shown in Fig. S4b. The intensities of the peaks showed decreasing trends as the reaction times increased. This result indicates that carbaryl molecules can be gradually absorbed on TiO2/ZnO/CQD under the darkness at 0–30 min, and rapidly degraded under UVA irradiation for another 90 min. The removal of carbaryl from water using composite photocatalysts has been reported in several publications. The multi-walled carbon nanotubes composite with TiO2 (MWNTs-TiO2) was developed. The removal of carbaryl was approximately 20 % and 30 % under UV–visible irradiation for 4 h in the presence of pristine TiO2 and MWNTs-TiO2, respectively [43]. A ternary C/ZnO/BiOI composite photocatalyst was developed. The removal of carbaryl was 62.9 % under simulated sunlight irradiation for 150 min [44]. The ZnO doping with water hyacinth-derived activated carbon (ZnO-AC) photocatalyst was also developed for the removal of carbaryl from water under sunlight irradiation. The degradation rate constant was 8.43 × 10−3 min−1, which is 1.5 times higher than that of the pristine ZnO [45].

Pseudo-first order kinetic model combined with method of initial rate is commonly applied for heterogeneous photocatalysis, as expressed in Eq. (1). The linear form of the integrated equation (Eq. (2)) is used for fitting the experimental data [37].

The coefficients of determination (R2) were in the range of 0.9836–0.9991. Kinetic parameters related to the performance of photocatalytic materials are listed in Table 3 . ce:section.ce:para:
The heterogeneous photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants in water depends on light intensity (I), surface area of photocatalyst (s), initial concentration of organic pollutants in water (C), and adsorption-desorption behaviors of the photocatalyst [48]. The functional equations of photocatalytic reaction rate (r) can be expressed as Eq. (4). K is a lumped of adsorption-desorption parameters. The empirical correlation based on influential parameters can be expressed as Eq. (5) [49].

The findings from the scavenger test demonstrate the predominant reactive species involved in the photocatalytic degradation mechanism assisted by the TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite. In Fig. S5 clearly the absence of any scavenger under the control condition, the system displayed a degradation efficiency of 99.01 %, thereby confirming the superior photocatalytic efficiency of the nanocomposite. However, the incorporation of IPA, proven to be a scavenger for hydroxyl radicals (•OH), resulted in a notable reduction in degradation efficiency, which decreased to approximately 24.6 %. The significant decrease observed underscores the role of hydroxyl radicals as the principal reactive species involved in the degradation process, attributable to their elevated oxidative potential and capacity for non-selective degradation of organic pollutants. The introduction of EDTA, identified as a scavenger for photogenerated holes (h+), resulted in a moderate decrease in degradation efficiency, which was measured at approximately 72.2 %. This observation indicates that the presence of holes contributes a supporting role within the degradation process. The findings regarding BQ, a known scavenger of superoxide radicals (•O2 −), indicated a reduction in degradation to 41.6 %. This suggests that while superoxide radicals play a secondary role in the degradation process, their influence is comparatively less significant than that of hydroxyl radicals. In the course of the investigation, the application of AgNO3, identified as an electron scavenger, yielded a degradation rate of 81.5 %. This finding indicates that the contribution of photogenerated electrons is limited, primarily serving to promote the generation of •O2 − radicals. The results indicate that hydroxyl radicals (•OH) represent the most active and predominant species within the photocatalytic degradation mechanism. Superoxide radicals (•O2 −) and photogenerated holes (h+) contribute to the process, albeit to a lesser extent.

In this study, the photocatalytic mechanism of the TiO2/ZnO/CQD under UVA irradiation is proposed, as displayed in Fig. 8 . The light source was UVA (365 nm), which can simultaneously excite TiO2, ZnO and CQD to generate photoexcited e− and h+. CQD served as electron donors. The potentials of the conduction band (CB) and valence band edges of TiO2/ZnO/CQD were determined using the Mulliken-Jaffe equation (Eqs. (9) and (10))

e is free electron energy on the hydrogen scale (4.5 eV), and Eg is the bandgap of semiconductor. The calculated CB potential of TiO2 and ZnO were − 0.21 V vs. normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) and − 0.44 V vs. NHE, respectively. The CB potential of TiO2 is less negative than the standard redox potentials of oxygen (
O 2

because their reduction potentials are insufficient.

OH −

OH −
to produce
•
OH which can further react with carbaryl molecules. In addition, the photoluminescence of CQD would happen with the maximum wavelength of 539 nm which corresponds to photon energy of 2.3 eV. However, the photon energy is not enough to excite TiO2/ZnO. This mechanism is consistent with the previous work, which reported the electron transfer at interface of mesoporous TiO2-modified ZnO quantum dot [47].

The pH is an important parameter in photocatalytic process affecting adsorption-desorption of organic pollutants onto catalyst surface. pH variation intensely affects the surface charge of photocatalyst, chemical species of carbaryl, and electrostatic reaction between carbaryl and catalyst surface. Carbaryl is a nonionic compound. Food and Agriculture Organization of United Nation (FAO) reported pKa of carbaryl at 10.4 ± 0.4. The dominant species will be anionic form if solution pH > pKa, and cationic form if solution pH < pKa [51]. Surface charge of the photocatalyst is usually positive at acidic pH and negative charged at alkaline pH [50]. The effect of initial pH on photocatalytic degradation of carbaryl was studied in the pH range of 3–11. In the dark adsorption stage (Fig. 9a), the removal percentage was found to be increased with increasing pH from 3 to 9 and decreased when pH was 11. This is because strong adsorption can be obtained as the charge polarity of carbaryl and photocatalyst surface are different [51]. From Fig. 9b, the highest overall removal efficiency was observed at pH of 7. It could be noted that the photocatalytic degradation of carbaryl was enhanced in the neutral medium rather than acidic or alkaline medium. Therefore, the optimum pH value was 7.

Reusability of the photocatalyst is one of the topics of concern. In each cycle, the experiment was initially run under darkness for 30 min, and then the UVA light bulbs were switched on for another 90 min. When each run was finished, the TiO2/ZnO/CQD powder was separable from the treated water. From Fig. 9c and d, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/ZnO/CQD powder slightly decreased from the first cycle to the fifth cycle. This is due to the loss of TiO2/ZnO/CQD powder approximately 25 % (0.025 g) in the process of separating and recycling. For this reason, the photocatalyst dose in each run decreased leading to the decrease in degradation efficiency. Additionally, XRD patterns were examined before and after the reusability test (Fig. S6). The TiO2/ZnO/CQD photocatalyst demonstrated a lack of significant alterations in its crystallographic phase when compared to the original catalyst. This observation substantiates its considerable structural stability and indicates a robust potential for practical applications. The findings underscore the material's potential to serve as a reliable and durable catalyst, thereby establishing it as a promising candidate for further development and potential commercial application in the field of environmental remediation. SEM images and EDS spectra of TiO2/ZnO/CQD before and after the reusability test are shown in Fig. S7. The surface morphology did not significantly change but the elemental composition of TiO2/ZnO/CQD after photocatalytic degradation of carbaryl in water for five cycles showed slightly higher carbon and oxygen contents (Table S1). This may be attributed to the residual adsorbed carbaryl molecules (C12H11NO2) on surface of the TiO2/ZnO/CQD after completing the fifth cycle.

The antibacterial effectiveness of the synthesized TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite was evaluated against Gram-positive and Gram-negative pathogenic microorganisms. Fig. 10a illustrates the antimicrobial properties of the synthesized TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite (50 and 75 μg/mL) and conventional antibiotics, as assessed by the disc diffusion method. The synthetic composite demonstrated broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against most of the tested bacterial pathogens (Fig. 10b). The most significant growth inhibition zone was observed for S. aureus (6 ± 0.2 mm), followed by E. coli (8 ± 0.1 mm) at a composite concentration of 75 μg/mL. The standard antibiotic ciprofloxacin exhibited bactericidal activity, with inhibition zones ranging from 12 mm to 13 mm. The findings of the antimicrobial effectiveness are comparable with recent studies on the antibacterial capabilities of TiO2/ZnO/CQD nanocomposite against drug-resistant microorganisms. The antibacterial action of TiO2/ZnO/CQD was predominantly due to the breakdown of cell membranes in Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Furthermore, cell elongation occurred because of DNA damage and the production of oxidative stress within the bacterial system, eventually leading to cell death. Micro and macro-sized particles were unable to rupture and penetrate the bacterial cell wall and DNA. The antibacterial action of TiO2/ZnO/CQD is often attributed to reactive oxygen species (ROS) disrupting membrane integrity, resulting in phospholipid peroxidation and, ultimately, cell death. These results are consistent with the previous studies [52,53]. The chitosan modified with montmorillonite/ZnO hydrogel nanocomposite exhibited good antimicrobial activity against both E. coli, and S. aureus [52]. The TiO2 thin films fabricated by the sol–gel dip-coating (SG) and low-temperature spray-coating (SP) techniques also effectively inhibited the proliferation of the E. coli cells under UVA [53].

CQD solution was synthesized from the spent coffee ground using the alkaline peroxide-assisted hydrothermal method. The composite photocatalysts were successfully prepared by a facile heat-treatment method, which were confirmed by TEM and XPS results. The ternary composite of TiO2/ZnO/CQD exhibited the highest efficiency of 99.01 % in the removal of carbaryl insecticide under UVA irradiation. The half-life time of carbaryl in the presence of TiO2/ZnO/CQD under UVA was 12.16 min. This is 5.5 and 1.7 times lower than that of pristine TiO2 and ZnO. The effect of initial concentration of carbaryl on the photocatalytic activity can be expressed by Langmuir-Hinshelwood rate equation. The TiO2/ZnO/CQD was separable from the treated water and reused for three cycles. The TiO2/ZnO/CQD had high bactericidal action for E. coli, and S. aureus bacterial strains, which was linked to the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) during light irradiation, which may destroy bacterial cell walls and impair critical cellular activities.
